planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings (which root very readily) or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; gumbo limbo is one of the easiest tropical trees to propagate — large fence posts and even entire trees can be transplanted and will root. It is highly salt, drought, and wind tolerant.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings or container trees in well-drained, rocky, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; gumbo limbo is nicknamed the 'tourist tree' because its distinctive reddish, peeling bark resembles a sunburned tourist. The shiny, exfoliating, copper-red outer bark peels off in thin sheets to reveal smooth new bark beneath — one of the most ornamentally interesting barks of any South Florida native. The tree is remarkably tough: hurricane-resistant due to flexible limbs, very tolerant of salt spray and coastal conditions, and one of the few South Florida hammock trees that can be grown from large cuttings — even cut limbs several inches in diameter, stuck in moist soil, will root readily. The red, fleshy fruits are eaten by numerous subtropical birds. A cornerstone of South Florida coastal hammock forest and an outstanding landscape tree for zone 10 coastal settings.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Florida strangler fig is native to subtropical hammocks and rockland habitats of south Florida and the Florida Keys. It typically starts as an epiphyte but can also be planted as a free-standing tree from container stock.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Florida strangler fig is the only native fig species of the continental United States (excluding Hawaii), naturally occurring in the subtropical hammocks and rockland of south Florida and the Keys. In nature it begins as an epiphyte — a seed dropped by a bird into a tree crotch germinates and sends aerial roots down to the soil that eventually engulf and 'strangle' the host tree. As a free-standing garden tree it simply grows as a magnificent, large-canopy evergreen. The small yellow-orange figs are eaten by an extraordinary variety of wildlife — dozens of bird species, Florida panthers, and raccoons — making the strangler fig one of the most ecologically productive trees in south Florida. The dense canopy provides important nesting habitat for raptors. A keystone species of the south Florida hammock ecosystem.

planting.note: Plant fresh seeds in spring in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; paradise tree is native to tropical lowland forests of Central and South America and requires frost-free conditions. It is being promoted as a multipurpose agroforestry and bioenergy tree in tropical regions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 10–12; paradise tree is a multipurpose tropical tree from Central and South America producing large, pinnately compound leaves that create a lush, tropical canopy effect. Small, fragrant, yellowish flowers produce clusters of small, dark purple olives containing seeds with 60–75% oil content — one of the highest vegetable oil contents of any plant, approaching the palmitic/oleic profile suitable for both food use and biodiesel production. The tree is extremely fast-growing (20+ feet per year in tropical conditions), drought-tolerant once established, and being actively developed as a low-input bioenergy crop in tropical developing countries. The red-and-yellow immature fruits and the dark purple ripe fruits are ornamentally attractive. Of very limited landscape use in the US outside zone 10–11 Florida, but grown as an agroforestry specimen.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky coastal soil in full sun; silver buttonwood grows naturally in coastal strand, mangrove fringes, and rocky coastal hammock of south Florida and the Caribbean, highly tolerant of salt spray, wind, and drought.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky coastal soil in full sun; silver buttonwood is a variety of buttonwood (Conocarpus erectus) distinguished by its striking silvery-gray foliage — the leaves are covered with fine, silky, silver-gray hairs (sericeus = silky) that give the entire plant a luminous, metallic quality unlike any other coastal plant. An outstanding ornamental for coastal landscapes in zone 10–11, it provides year-round silver foliage color with outstanding resistance to salt spray, drought, wind, and shallow rocky soils. Extremely tolerant of coastal exposure and one of the most salt-tolerant broadleaf trees for South Florida coastal plantings. Widely used as a coastal hedge, specimen shrub, or small tree in South Florida landscaping. The small, rounded flower clusters and button-like fruiting heads give the buttonwoods their common name. A native of coastal hammocks and mangrove transitions.

common_name: Tung Oil Tree (China Wood Oil Tree)

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist soil in full sun; tung oil tree was extensively planted in the Gulf South from the 1930s–1950s as a commercial oil crop. It tolerates mild frosts but is killed back to the ground by hard freezes in zone 8, often resprouting from roots.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; tung oil tree is a Chinese tree cultivated throughout zone 8–11 for its seeds, which yield tung oil — a fast-drying, water-resistant drying oil used extensively in paints, varnishes, wood preservatives, and wood finishing products. The spring flower display is spectacular — large clusters of white flowers with red or pink veining cover the bare branches before leaf emergence, followed by bold, large, heart-shaped leaves. The golf-ball-sized fruits contain 3–7 seeds with 50–60% oil content. The entire plant is poisonous if consumed — the seeds, leaves, and oil are toxic. The Gulf South has abandoned remnant tung orchards from a failed commercial industry of the mid-20th century. Now a naturalized, occasionally invasive ornamental/landscape tree notable for its spring flower show. Caution: all plant parts are toxic.
